# Verify that git checkout triggers the smudge filter, which reveals the secret
# to its plaintext path as a side effect - specifically when checking out an
# older commit whose encrypted blob differs from the current working tree.

exec git init
exec git config user.email test@sesam.dev
exec git config user.name 'Sesam Test'

exec sesam init --user admin
exec git add -A
exec git commit -m 'init sesam'

# Sanity-check: smudge filter is configured to use sesam.
exec git config --local filter.sesam-filter.smudge
stdout 'sesam smudge'

# Long-running filter process is configured for git 2.11+; the legacy
# smudge/clean keys remain as a fallback for older git versions.
exec git config --local filter.sesam-filter.process
stdout 'sesam smudge'

# Modify the secret and seal it, then commit - this produces a second blob.
cp modified_readme.md README.md
exec sesam seal
exec git add -A
exec git commit -m 'update readme'

# Confirm the working tree now contains the modified content.
exec cat README.md
stdout 'modified readme'
! stdout 'sesam bootstrap'

# Check out the OLD encrypted object from the first commit.
# Git calls smudge with the old blob; smudge must reveal the original plaintext.
exec git checkout HEAD~1 -- .sesam/objects/README.md.sesam

# README.md must now have the original content from the first commit.
exec cat README.md
stdout 'sesam bootstrap'
! stdout 'modified readme'

-- modified_readme.md --
# modified readme

This is the new content after the change.
